title: String and text processing

canonicalExplanation.summary: Java String values are immutable sequences of UTF-16 code units; content operations return new values, StringBuilder supports repeated construction, and equals compares content.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A parser uses equals for tokens, strip for surrounding whitespace, and a StringBuilder to assemble an unknown number of output fragments. Your goal is to implement text parsing and construction while avoiding reference-equality and concatenation pitfalls. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Loops and branching; pf abstraction calls; Method contracts and parameters; Arrays and multidimensional arrays. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Java String values are immutable sequences of UTF-16 code units; content operations return new values, StringBuilder supports repeated construction, and equals compares content. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Reference identity differs from content equality, code units differ from Unicode code points, locale affects some transformations, and repeated concatenation in a loop can allocate excessively. The learner must reject this documented misconception: The == operator compares String text, and one char always represents one complete Unicode character.

includes: Use immutable strings, builders and Unicode-aware operations appropriately.
Implement text parsing and construction while avoiding reference-equality and concatenation pitfalls.
excludes: Reference identity differs from content equality, code units differ from Unicode code points, locale affects some transformations, and repeated concatenation in a loop can allocate excessively.

outcomes: Implement text parsing and construction while avoiding reference-equality and concatenation pitfalls.

examples.title: String and text processing: direct language case
examples.situation: A parser uses equals for tokens, strip for surrounding whitespace, and a StringBuilder to assemble an unknown number of output fragments.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A parser uses equals for tokens, strip for surrounding whitespace, and a StringBuilder to assemble an unknown number of output fragments.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Use equals for String content and code-point-aware operations when Unicode characters matter; reserve == for reference identity checks.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Use equals for String content and code-point-aware operations when Unicode characters matter; reserve == for reference identity checks.

examples.title: String and text processing: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A display-length check counts code points rather than char units so a supplementary symbol is not treated as two user characters.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A display-length check counts code points rather than char units so a supplementary symbol is not treated as two user characters.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Reference identity differs from content equality, code units differ from Unicode code points, locale affects some transformations, and repeated concatenation in a loop can allocate excessively.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: The == operator compares String text, and one char always represents one complete Unicode character.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement text parsing and construction while avoiding reference-equality and concatenation pitfalls.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: The == operator compares String text, and one char always represents one complete Unicode character.
misconceptions.correction: Use equals for String content and code-point-aware operations when Unicode characters matter; reserve == for reference identity checks.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: The == operator compares String text, and one char always represents one complete Unicode character.

analogy.example: a set of labeled workstations that accept copies of request values and process fixed trays or immutable text tickets can introduce String and text processing, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: reasoning about method contracts, scope, arrays, and strings
limitations: reference values can still alias mutable objects and Unicode or overload rules have no exact workstation equivalent

recap.summary: You should now be able to implement text parsing and construction while avoiding reference-equality and concatenation pitfalls. Keep this boundary in view: Reference identity differs from content equality, code units differ from Unicode code points, locale affects some transformations, and repeated concatenation in a loop can allocate excessively.
